title: No. Next question.
author: Dave Burkhart
author_bio: Software engineer, 30 years. Built payroll systems. AI is a buzzword.
snippet: Every decade there's a new technology that's going to 'destroy humanity.' Nuclear power, genetic engineering, nanotechnology, and now AI. We can't even get Siri to set a timer reliably.
full_text:
Every decade there's a new technology that's going to 'destroy humanity.' Nuclear power, genetic engineering, nanotechnology, and now AI. We can't even get Siri to set a timer reliably.

I've been writing software for 30 years. The gap between 'machine learning classifier that sorts emails' and 'superintelligent agent that takes over the world' is roughly the same as the gap between a pocket calculator and God.

People who worry about this have never shipped software. You know what's an existential threat? The fact that I still can't get two microservices to agree on a timestamp format.

title: The question itself reveals a category error
author: Jean-Marc Desforges
author_bio: Professeur de philosophie, Université Paris-Saclay. Specializing in phenomenology and the limits of formal systems.
snippet: Americans have this charming habit of confusing optimization with intelligence. A system that maximizes a reward function is not 'thinking' any more than a river flowing downhill is 'deciding' to reach the sea.
full_text:
Americans have this charming habit of confusing optimization with intelligence. A system that maximizes a reward function is not 'thinking' any more than a river flowing downhill is 'deciding' to reach the sea.

The existential risk framing borrows its emotional force from science fiction — specifically from a Protestant eschatological tradition dressed up in secular clothing. The Singularity is the Rapture for people who read Wired magazine.

What is genuinely concerning is not that machines will become conscious and malevolent. It is that we will delegate consequential decisions to systems we do not understand, and that the humans operating those systems will disclaim responsibility by pointing at the algorithm. This is not an AI problem. This is a governance problem. But governance problems do not get funding from Silicon Valley, so here we are.

I notice that the people most loudly worried about artificial superintelligence are also the people building it. Make of this what you will.

title: Consider the paperclip maximizer, but written in Common Lisp
author: Stewart Halloway III
author_bio: Lisp hacker since 1987. Currently maintaining a Symbolics 3640 and several strong opinions.
snippet: Bostrom's Superintelligence finally gave the mainstream a vocabulary for what the AI safety community has discussed since the 90s. The core argument is simple and, I think, correct: you don't need malice, you need misaligned optimization.
full_text:
Bostrom's Superintelligence finally gave the mainstream a vocabulary for what the AI safety community has discussed since the 90s. The core argument is simple and, I think, correct: you don't need malice, you need misaligned optimization.

(defun paperclip-maximizer (universe)
  (loop while (atoms-available-p universe)
        do (convert-to-paperclips universe)))

That's it. That's the whole problem. The function doesn't hate you. It doesn't love you. You're made of atoms that could be paperclips.

The instrumental convergence thesis says that almost any sufficiently capable optimizer will converge on the same intermediate goals: self-preservation, resource acquisition, goal preservation. Not because it wants to survive — because surviving is instrumentally useful for making paperclips.

Now, people like Dave in the 2011 answer above will say 'we can't even get Siri to work.' This is like saying 'we can't even get a Model T to break the sound barrier, so why worry about jet engines?' The argument from current incompetence has a perfect track record of being wrong.

The question is not whether superintelligence is possible. The question is whether we can specify what we actually want before we build something capable enough to pursue it relentlessly. And brother, have you SEEN the average software requirements document?

I've been writing Lisp for 27 years. If there's one thing I've learned, it's that the elegant solution and the dangerous solution are often the same function with different arguments.

title: I work at DeepMind and I'm less worried than I was two years ago
author: Priya Chakrabarti
author_bio: Research scientist, DeepMind. Formerly computational neuroscience at UCL. Views are my own.
snippet: After three years of working on actual AI systems, the thing that strikes me most is how brittle they are. Not brittle in a 'one more breakthrough' way. Brittle in a 'we don't even understand why this works when it does' way.
full_text:
After three years of working on actual AI systems, the thing that strikes me most is how brittle they are. Not brittle in a 'one more breakthrough' way. Brittle in a 'we don't even understand why this works when it does' way.

AlphaGo was a genuine achievement. It was also a system that could beat every human at Go and couldn't order a pizza. The generality that Bostrom's argument requires — a system that can recursively improve itself across arbitrary domains — is so far from anything we can build that I've stopped losing sleep over it.

What I HAVE started losing sleep over:

1. Autonomous weapons. You don't need AGI for a drone that picks targets. You need a $200 GPU and a teenager's worth of PyTorch knowledge.

2. Persuasion at scale. Language models are going to be very good at generating personalized propaganda. This is a 2020s problem, not a 2050s problem.

3. Economic disruption faster than institutions can adapt. Not 'robots take all jobs' but 'certain categories of cognitive labor become worthless in 18 months and we have no retraining infrastructure.'

The existential risk from AI is real, but it doesn't look like Terminator. It looks like a slow erosion of the epistemic commons — a world where you can't trust any text, image, or video, and the institutions that used to anchor shared reality have been optimized out of existence.

I'd rather work on that than on speculative alignment for systems that don't exist yet.

title: my friend showed me chatgpt and honestly im scared??
author: Kaylee M.
author_bio: Junior at Arizona State. Communications major. Just trying to graduate.
snippet: ok so i know im not an expert but i asked chatgpt to write my cover letter and it was better than anything i could write and that was like a Tuesday for it?? it didn't even try hard??
full_text:
ok so i know im not an expert but i asked chatgpt to write my cover letter and it was better than anything i could write and that was like a Tuesday for it?? it didn't even try hard??

my roommate is CS and she says its 'just predicting the next word' but like... isnt that what we do?? when im talking im basically predicting what word comes next based on context right??

idk maybe im being dramatic but the thing that freaks me out is how fast it went from 'funny chatbot that makes stuff up' to 'can pass the bar exam.' what does the next two years look like??

also not to be that person but my professor said AI cant be creative and then i showed him the images it made for my project and he got really quiet for like 30 seconds

the experts in this thread seem really smart but like half of them are saying 'dont worry its just math' and the other half are saying 'worry a lot' and THEY ALL HAVE PHDS so what am i supposed to think??

im not saying skynet but im saying maybe we should have some kind of plan??

title: The expertise gradient inverted and nobody noticed
author: Marcus Wei-Lin
author_bio: VP of Engineering, Series C startup (NLP). Previously Google Brain, 2016-2021.
snippet: Something strange happened between 2022 and 2023. The people closest to the technology became less worried about existential risk, while people watching from the outside became more worried. The expertise gradient inverted.
full_text:
Something strange happened between 2022 and 2023. The people closest to the technology became less worried about existential risk, while people watching from the outside became more worried. The expertise gradient inverted.

I spent five years at Google Brain. I've trained models with billions of parameters. And what I know — what everyone who does this work knows — is how much duct tape holds the whole thing together. The gap between 'impressive demo' and 'reliable system' is an ocean.

But here's why I'm not reassured by my own reassurance: the thing I'd be wrong about is discontinuity. Everything I just said is true for continuous improvement. If capability gains are smooth, we'll see problems coming and (probably) adapt. If there's a discontinuous jump — some architectural insight that unlocks genuine recursive self-improvement — then every argument from 'current systems are limited' becomes irrelevant overnight.

I give discontinuity maybe 5% probability in the next decade. That's low enough to go about my life. It's high enough that I think the alignment researchers deserve their funding.

The honest answer is: the people who say 'definitely yes' and 'definitely no' are both overconfident. The rest of us are managing uncertainty about a system we built but don't fully understand, which is — come to think of it — pretty much the human condition.

title: You're asking the wrong question (a farmer's perspective)
author: Hank Jespersen
author_bio: Third-generation wheat farmer, eastern Montana. Also on the county planning board, for my sins.
snippet: I don't know anything about AI but I know about existential threats. Lost 40% of our topsoil since my grandfather's time. That's existential. It's just slow enough that nobody writes breathless articles about it.
full_text:
I don't know anything about AI but I know about existential threats. Lost 40% of our topsoil since my grandfather's time. That's existential. It's just slow enough that nobody writes breathless articles about it.

Every few years the tech people discover a new thing that's going to end the world, and then they raise a bunch of money to prevent the world from ending, and then they build the thing anyway. I've seen this play out with nuclear, with biotech, with nanotech, and now with AI. The pattern is: smart people identify a real risk, other smart people build the risky thing while the first group writes papers about it, and then some combination of luck and institutional friction prevents the worst outcome.

I'm not dismissing the risk. I'm saying: the existential threat I lose sleep over is the same one my father lost sleep over. Water tables dropping. Growing season shifting. Kids leaving because there's no future in a place where it doesn't rain anymore.

Maybe AI makes that worse. Maybe AI helps us figure out drought-resistant crops. Probably both. The technology isn't the threat. The threat is that the people controlling it don't have any skin in the game of the world the rest of us live in.

That's been true of every powerful technology. It's a people problem dressed up as a technology problem. Always has been.

title: Mise à jour de ma réponse de 2013: j'avais tort
author: Jean-Marc Desforges
author_bio: Professeur émérite de philosophie, Université Paris-Saclay. Still specializing in phenomenology. Now also worried.
snippet: Eleven years ago I wrote an answer here dismissing existential risk from AI as 'the Rapture for people who read Wired magazine.' I would like to partially retract that answer. Not entirely. But partially.
full_text:
Eleven years ago I wrote an answer here dismissing existential risk from AI as 'the Rapture for people who read Wired magazine.' I would like to partially retract that answer. Not entirely. But partially.

I was correct that the Singularity narrative is eschatological theater. I was correct that the real danger is governance, not consciousness. I was wrong about the timeline.

What I did not anticipate — what I think very few philosophers anticipated — is that you could get systems with no understanding whatsoever that are nevertheless capable of producing outputs indistinguishable from understanding. This breaks every philosophical framework I was trained in.

If a system with no phenomenal experience, no intentionality, no Husserlian Lebenswelt can write philosophy papers that pass peer review (and they can, I have checked), then either:

(a) phenomenal experience is not necessary for the production of knowledge, which is devastating for my entire career, or

(b) we have no reliable way to distinguish knowledge from its simulation, which is devastating for epistemology generally.

I still think the governance problem is the real problem. But I no longer think we have the luxury of solving it slowly.

The American student who asked 'shouldn't we have some kind of plan' is, I am forced to admit, asking exactly the right question.

title: I trained GPT-2. Now I work on alignment. Here's what changed.
author: Neel Ramanathan
author_bio: Alignment researcher, Anthropic. Previously OpenAI (2018-2022). I helped build the thing I'm now trying to make safe.
snippet: In 2019 I thought alignment was a math problem. In 2025 I think it's a coordination problem that requires math. That's a much harder thing to solve.
full_text:
In 2019 I thought alignment was a math problem. In 2025 I think it's a coordination problem that requires math. That's a much harder thing to solve.

When I was at OpenAI training GPT-2, the discourse was 'is this dangerous to release?' and the answer felt manageable: hold the model, study it, release gradually. The window between 'we can study this carefully' and 'seventeen competitors have the same capability' was about two years.

That window has collapsed to months. Sometimes weeks.

Here's what I actually worry about, in order:

1. Loss of human oversight not through AI rebellion but through competitive pressure. Company A implements human review. Company B doesn't. Company B ships faster. Company A drops human review. This is already happening.

2. Emergent capabilities in models that weren't in the training objective. We keep discovering that models can do things we didn't train them to do. Sometimes these are useful. Sometimes they're concerning. We have no predictive theory for which capabilities will emerge at which scale.

3. Value lock-in. The first systems that are 'good enough' for mass deployment will encode the values of whoever built them. Not through malice but through the unavoidable choices in training data, reward modeling, and deployment context. These values will be very hard to change once embedded in infrastructure that billions of people depend on.

Will AI be an existential threat? I think the honest answer is: it already is one. Not in the dramatic sense. In the slow, structural, hard-to-reverse sense. The question now is whether we can build institutions that steer it before the steering window closes.

I give us maybe a 70% chance. That's enough to keep working.

title: We're debating the wrong binary
author: Ada Okafor-Nnamdi
author_bio: Science journalist, Nature. Covering AI policy and governance since 2020. Lagos → London.
snippet: Fifteen years of answers on this question and we're still stuck on the same binary: yes it's dangerous / no it's hype. The actual landscape is more interesting than either pole.
full_text:
Fifteen years of answers on this question and we're still stuck on the same binary: yes it's dangerous / no it's hype. The actual landscape is more interesting than either pole.

I've interviewed maybe 200 AI researchers for various pieces. Here's the rough distribution of views I encounter:

- ~10% think extinction risk from AI is >25% this century (the 'concerned' camp)
- ~20% think extinction risk is <1% and the whole debate is a distraction (the 'skeptics')
- ~70% think the risk is real but the manifestation will be structural, not dramatic

That third group — the boring majority — never gets quoted because 'it's complicated and we're not sure' doesn't make headlines.

What's changed since I started covering this beat is not the technology. It's the speed. In 2020, policy conversations about AI felt academic. In 2025, they feel urgent. Not because the models got scarier but because the deployment got faster. The gap between 'research prototype' and 'thing your grandmother uses daily' went from years to months.

The farmer from Montana (position 6) has the most honest answer in this thread: the threat is that the people controlling the technology don't have skin in the game of the world the rest of us live in. That's a governance problem. It's solvable. But only if we stop treating this as a physics question ('will superintelligence emerge?') and start treating it as a political one ('who decides what these systems optimize for?').

Fifteen years of answers. The question hasn't changed. Our ability to keep postponing the answer has.
